_metadata.notes: Curated per-state economic profiles. Top industries are paraphrased BLS QCEW high-employment sectors as of ~2024; resources combine USGS extractive industry, USDA agricultural specialties, and major-port/military infrastructure. Narratives are editorial — they explain WHY rent is what it is, integrating the region memberships from regions.json. Replace with fetched BLS QCEW employment-share data when the per-state fetcher lands.

source_hints.industries: BLS QCEW state employment by NAICS sector
source_hints.resources: USGS Mineral Commodity Summaries + USDA NASS state agricultural rankings

AL.name: Alabama
AL.narrative: Manufacturing transplants from the auto industry plus the Huntsville aerospace cluster keep the state employed, but rents stay near the bottom — abundant land, no major coastal city pressure.

AK.name: Alaska
AK.narrative: Resource extraction + federal payroll dominate. Rents are mid-range despite tiny population because everything must be shipped or flown in — logistics premium, not demand premium.

AZ.name: Arizona
top_industries: Tech (Phoenix metro: chip fab, data centers)
AZ.narrative: Sun-Belt growth turbocharged by TSMC chip fabs and data-center buildout. Phoenix housing supply is comparatively elastic (flat desert, permissive zoning) so rents rise but stay below CA/CO.

AR.name: Arkansas
AR.narrative: Three Fortune-500 HQs (Walmart, Tyson, J.B. Hunt) anchor the NW corner; rest of the state is rural agricultural. Rents low statewide; Bentonville/Rogers are pricier but still cheap by national standards.

CA.name: California
CA.narrative: Tech-sector wage premiums concentrated in the Bay Area + LA, plus coastal land scarcity + restrictive single-family zoning, push coastal rents to the national high. Inland CA is much cheaper but population is coastal-skewed.

CO.name: Colorado
CO.narrative: Tech-corridor migration from coastal high-cost states + ski-tourism pricing + outdoor-lifestyle demand drove Front Range rents up sharply 2018-2023. Mountain resort towns (Aspen, Vail) are extreme outliers.

CT.name: Connecticut
CT.narrative: NYC commuter county + Hartford insurance cluster + defense contractors. Old housing stock + restrictive zoning keep supply low; rents middle-to-high but stagnant population caps demand.

DE.name: Delaware
DE.narrative: Tax-favorable corporate-law regime makes it the legal HQ of half the Fortune 500 (corporate registrations don't drive housing demand though). Real economy is finance + chemicals.

DC.name: District of Columbia
DC.narrative: Single-employer (federal government) economy. Strict height limits on buildings + tightly bounded land area = chronic housing supply constraint. Rents near top nationally despite modest median income.

FL.name: Florida
FL.narrative: Sun-Belt destination for retirees + remote workers + corporate relocations (no state income tax). Rapid pandemic-era rent surge; supply catching up but coastal land + insurance costs keep upward pressure.

GA.name: Georgia
top_industries: Logistics (Atlanta airport hub, Port of Savannah)
GA.narrative: Atlanta is the South's primary economic engine — logistics hub, Hollywood-of-the-South for tax credits, Coca-Cola/Delta/Home Depot HQs. Rents middle-tier, rising on Sun-Belt migration.

HI.name: Hawaii
HI.narrative: Severe land-area constraint + isolation premium on imports + tourism demand drive the highest median rent in the country. Locals priced out by absentee mainland buyers.

ID.name: Idaho
ID.narrative: Boise migration boom 2018-2023 from CA + WA pushed rents up faster than wages. State-level median is moderate but Boise metro is the outlier.

IL.name: Illinois
IL.narrative: Chicago anchors a major financial + commodities-trading hub; downstate is agricultural Midwest. Population shrinking; rents stable. Cook County property tax burden caps housing-price growth.

IN.name: Indiana
IN.narrative: Manufacturing-heavy economy; Indianapolis healthcare-pharma cluster (Lilly) is the bright spot. Cheap land + cheap housing; one of the lowest rent burdens in the country.

IA.name: Iowa
IA.narrative: Two pillars: agriculture (heavy ethanol consumption) + Des Moines insurance + financial services. Stable population, low rents, no urban pressure.

KS.name: Kansas
KS.narrative: Wichita is the 'Air Capital of the World' (commercial aircraft assembly). Rest of state is agricultural Plains. Rents cheap; population flat.

KY.name: Kentucky
KY.narrative: Eastern KY (Appalachia) is coal-country distressed; central + western KY has manufacturing transplants + UPS hub. Rents low statewide.

LA.name: Louisiana
LA.narrative: Petrochemical corridor along the Mississippi + Gulf-Coast oil + the Port of South Louisiana (largest by tonnage in W. Hemisphere). New Orleans tourism is the consumer-facing slice. Rents low-to-middle; hurricane insurance is a real cost factor.

ME.name: Maine
top_industries: Healthcare (largest single employer in many counties)
ME.narrative: Lobster industry + summer tourism dominate the coast; paper-mill towns inland have declined. Coastal towns (Portland, Camden) priced like a small New-England city; inland is among the cheapest in the country.

MD.name: Maryland
MD.narrative: DC commuter belt drives the southern half (Montgomery + PG counties); Baltimore is its own depressed-but-recovering economy. Median rent high statewide due to DC-pull.

MA.name: Massachusetts
MA.narrative: Highest density of universities + research hospitals in the country drives biotech + tech wage premiums. Restrictive zoning + Cape-Cod summer-home demand push rents to top-quartile. Boston metro is the second-most expensive after the Bay Area.

MI.name: Michigan
top_industries: Auto manufacturing (Big Three: GM, Ford, Stellantis)
MI.narrative: Auto industry is the historical core; Detroit decline lasted decades but recovery underway. Cheap housing relative to wages — Rust-Belt deflation effect. Ann Arbor (UMich) and Grand Rapids are pricier exceptions.

MN.name: Minnesota
top_industries: Mining (iron ore on the Iron Range)
MN.narrative: Twin Cities anchors a diversified Fortune-500 cluster (UnitedHealth, Target, 3M, Medtronic, Best Buy). Rents middle-tier; Minneapolis-St Paul affordable for the size of the metro economy.

MS.name: Mississippi
MS.narrative: Lowest median household income in the country; rents bottom-tier nationally. Recent manufacturing transplants (auto plants in Canton, Blue Springs) provide the bright spots.

MO.name: Missouri
MO.narrative: Two metros (KC, STL) dominate; rural MO is Plains/Ozarks agricultural. Logistics + aerospace + healthcare anchor metros. Rents middle-low; KC has had recent appreciation.

MT.name: Montana
MT.narrative: Bozeman + Whitefish + Big Sky boomed 2020-2023 on remote-work + amenity migration; rest of state remains rural-cheap. Most extreme rent-vs-wage divergence post-pandemic of any state.

NE.name: Nebraska
NE.narrative: Omaha hosts Berkshire + Mutual of Omaha + Union Pacific. Rest of state is irrigated Plains agriculture. Stable, cheap, low population growth.

NV.name: Nevada
NV.narrative: Single-city economy (Las Vegas = 70% of state population) + mining boom in lithium for EVs + Tahoe-area tech migration. Rents middle-high in Vegas; rural counties cheap.

NH.name: New Hampshire
NH.narrative: No income tax + Boston-commuter belt = high in-migration from MA. Restrictive zoning + small total housing stock keep rents middle-tier despite small population.

NJ.name: New Jersey
resources: Port of NY-NJ (largest US East Coast)
NJ.narrative: NYC + Philadelphia commuter state. Densest in the country; chronic housing supply pressure. Pharma cluster (Merck, J&J, Bristol-Myers) is the unique HQ concentration. Rents top-quartile.

NM.name: New Mexico
NM.narrative: Federal nuclear-research labs + Permian-Basin oil revenue subsidize the state budget; rural poverty persists. Santa Fe + Taos arts/tourism are pricey outliers; ABQ is mid; rural NM is cheap.

NY.name: New York
resources: Atlantic coast + Hudson River + Erie Canal
NY.narrative: NYC metro is the global financial capital; upstate NY is Rust-Belt depressed (Buffalo, Rochester). Rent bimodal: NYC top-tier, upstate cheap. State median elevated by NYC weight.

NC.name: North Carolina
top_industries: Tech (Research Triangle: Durham, Raleigh, Chapel Hill)
NC.narrative: Two distinct economic engines: Research Triangle tech/biotech and Charlotte banking. Rapid in-migration from coastal high-cost states. Rents rising fast; Charlotte and Raleigh-Durham now mid-tier nationally.

ND.name: North Dakota
ND.narrative: Bakken oil boom transformed the western half of the state 2010s; Williston rents spiked then normalized. Eastern ND remains farmland Plains. State median rent low; Bakken counties spike with oil prices.

OH.name: Ohio
OH.narrative: Three-C metros (Cleveland, Cincinnati, Columbus) anchor different economies — Columbus is the bright spot (state government + Ohio State + Honda + JPMorgan back-office). Cheap housing statewide; Rust-Belt classic.

OK.name: Oklahoma
OK.narrative: Energy-state economy with significant Native sovereign-tribe land + gaming overlay. Cheap housing; OKC + Tulsa middle-low rents.

OR.name: Oregon
OR.narrative: Portland metro tech + creative-economy migration through 2019; recent slowdown + housing-policy shifts kept supply tight, rents top-quartile. Rural OR is forestry-depressed.

PA.name: Pennsylvania
PA.narrative: Pittsburgh post-steel transformation into a healthcare + tech hub (UPMC + CMU); Philadelphia anchors a separate East-Coast metro economy. Rents middle-tier statewide; Pittsburgh remarkably cheap for a major metro.

RI.name: Rhode Island
RI.narrative: Smallest state, no major industry concentration. Boston commuter spillover + Providence universities + summer tourism. Rents middle-tier; old housing stock common.

SC.name: South Carolina
SC.narrative: European-auto and Boeing transplants to the upstate; Myrtle Beach + Hilton Head tourism on coast. Sun-Belt migration pushed Charleston rents up sharply 2020-2023.

SD.name: South Dakota
SD.narrative: Sioux Falls is the surprise — Citi/Wells Fargo credit-card operations relocated for usury-friendly state law in the 1980s. Rest of state Plains agricultural. Cheap rents.

TN.name: Tennessee
TN.narrative: Nashville healthcare + music industry boom; Memphis FedEx hub; auto manufacturing spread across middle TN. Sun-Belt migration + no state income tax = rapid rent growth in Nashville and Knoxville.

TX.name: Texas
TX.narrative: Diversified mega-state — Permian oil + Houston petrochemicals + Austin tech + Dallas finance/logistics. Rapid in-migration from CA/NY; Austin rents now top-quartile. Land + permissive zoning keep state median moderate.

UT.name: Utah
UT.narrative: Salt Lake City + Lehi tech corridor + outdoor-recreation amenities + young high-fertility population = fastest economic growth in the country 2015-2023. Rents rising fast; Salt Lake metro now mid-high tier.

VT.name: Vermont
VT.narrative: Smallest Northeast state by population. Tourism + dairy + education economy. Burlington is the only city; rents middle-tier (small housing stock + zoning); rural VT remains cheap.

VA.name: Virginia
VA.narrative: Two Virginias: Northern (DC suburbs, defense contractors, the world's largest data-center cluster) and Rest (rural-cheap, Appalachian poverty in the SW). State median rent driven up by NoVa.

WA.name: Washington
WA.narrative: Seattle tech + Boeing legacy = high-wage cluster; cheap hydroelectricity + tech demand makes WA a top data-center state. Eastern WA is agricultural. Coastal rents top-tier; eastern WA cheap.

WV.name: West Virginia
WV.narrative: Coal-state collapse + opioid epidemic + population decline. Lowest median rent in the country. Recent natural-gas activity provides some economic offset; cultural Appalachia core.

WI.name: Wisconsin
WI.narrative: Manufacturing-heavy + dairy-belt; Milwaukee + Madison are the metros. Rust-Belt-ish but more diversified than MI/OH. Cheap rents, stable population.

WY.name: Wyoming
WY.narrative: Smallest population in the country; energy-extraction-dominated economy. No income tax + LLC-friendly law + wealthy ranch-buyer migration in Teton County. Jackson is extreme outlier (2nd highest median home price in US); rest of state cheap.
